incorrects.description: Student selected Option B: reversed the numbers, showing 3 groups of 5 instead of 5 groups of 3

steps.text: The first number tells you how many groups. Look at the expression. How many groups do you need?

incorrects.description: Student selected Option C: scattered items with no equal group structure

params.description: Option A highlights showing clear group structure.

steps.text: Multiplication shows equal groups. The items need to be organized into groups with the same number in each. Which picture shows that?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bags in Option A highlight one by one showing 5 groups.

steps.text: Let me show you. The first number is groups. 5 times 3 means 5 groups. Count the bags in Option A.

params.description: Items in one bag highlight showing 3 items.

steps.text: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five bags. The second number is how many in each group. Look inside one bag. 1, 2, 3. Three items. Option A shows 5 groups with 3 in each. That's what 5 times 3 means.
